title: Print Name N Times
description: Given a name and a positive integer n, print the name exactly n times using recursion.
outputFormat: Print the name on n lines
intendedApproachSummary: Stop when the printed count reaches n. Otherwise print once and recurse on the smaller remaining count.
wrongApproachHints: If you use a loop, you miss the point of the exercise.
Make sure the recursive call moves toward the base case.
hints: Think about what the function should do once.
Reduce the remaining count by 1.
Stop when no prints remain.

examples.explanation: The function prints Alex once per recursive call until the count becomes 0.

title: Print Numbers from 1 to N
description: Print all integers from 1 to n using recursion.
outputFormat: Print numbers from 1 to n in increasing order
intendedApproachSummary: Carry the current number as a parameter. Print it, then recurse with the next value until the current number passes n.
wrongApproachHints: Printing after the recursive call changes the order.
Do not forget to stop once the current value exceeds n.
hints: Use a helper with the current index.
Base case should stop after n.
Decide whether to print before or after recursion based on order.

examples.explanation: Each call prints one value and moves to the next integer.

title: Factorial of N
description: Return the factorial of n using recursion.
intendedApproachSummary: Use the identity n! = n * (n - 1)! with a base case of 0! = 1 and 1! = 1.
wrongApproachHints: If you forget the base case, the recursion never stops.
Return the recursive result after multiplying by n.
hints: What is the smallest factorial you already know?
Reduce n by 1 each time.

title: Sum of First N Numbers
description: Find the sum of the first n natural numbers using recursion.
intendedApproachSummary: Either carry the running sum as a parameter or return n + sum(n - 1).
wrongApproachHints: Do not combine both parameterized and functional styles incorrectly.
A wrong base case will miss 0 or 1.
hints: Try both recursion styles once.
What should sum(0) return?

title: Power Function
description: Given integers a and b, compute a raised to the power b using recursion.
intendedApproachSummary: Return 1 when the exponent is 0. Otherwise return a multiplied by the result for exponent b - 1.
wrongApproachHints: Returning a when b = 0 is incorrect.
Make sure the exponent decreases every call.
hints: What is any number to the power 0?
Build the answer one multiplication at a time.

title: Fibonacci Number
description: Return the nth Fibonacci number using plain recursion first, so you can observe repeated calls.
intendedApproachSummary: Use fib(n) = fib(n - 1) + fib(n - 2) with base cases for 0 and 1.
wrongApproachHints: Without both base cases the recursion breaks.
This version is intentionally inefficient to teach tree recursion.
hints: What are the first two Fibonacci numbers?
Each state branches into two smaller states.

examples.explanation: The sequence is 0, 1, 1, 2, 3, 5, 8.

title: Palindrome Check
description: Check whether a string is a palindrome using recursion.
outputFormat: Return true if s is a palindrome, otherwise false
intendedApproachSummary: Compare the left and right characters, then recurse inward on the smaller substring range.
wrongApproachHints: A wrong stopping condition causes index errors.
Stop once the pointers cross or meet.
hints: Compare the ends first.
Move both pointers inward.

examples.explanation: The outer letters match at every step until the middle is reached.

title: Reverse a String Recursively
description: Return the reverse of a string using recursion.
intendedApproachSummary: Return the reverse of the smaller suffix and append the current character at the end, or swap characters recursively using indices.
wrongApproachHints: Be careful with substring boundaries.
Appending in the wrong order returns the original string.
hints: Shrink the string from the front.
Think about what reverse("bc") should return before adding 'a'.

examples.explanation: The last character ends up first after recursive unwinding.

title: Sum of Digits
description: Given a non-negative integer n, return the sum of its digits using recursion.
intendedApproachSummary: Take the last digit with n % 10, then recurse on n / 10 until the number becomes 0.
wrongApproachHints: The stopping condition for 0 is important.
Do not forget to add the current last digit to the recursive result.
hints: Split into last digit and remaining prefix.
Keep reducing n by dividing by 10.

title: Count Digits Recursively
description: Count the number of decimal digits in n using recursion.
outputFormat: Return the number of digits in n
intendedApproachSummary: Each division by 10 removes one digit. Recurse until the number becomes smaller than 10.
wrongApproachHints: 0 has one digit, not zero digits.
Make sure the recursion shrinks n each time.
hints: What is the answer for a single digit?
Every recursive step removes one digit.

examples.explanation: 5072 contains four digits.

title: Recursive Binary Search
description: Given a sorted array and a target value, return the index of the target using recursive binary search.
outputFormat: Return the index of target, or -1 if it is absent
edgeCases: Target at the first or last index
intendedApproachSummary: Choose a middle index, compare against the target, and recurse only into the half that can still contain the answer.
wrongApproachHints: Using binary search requires sorted input.
Watch your left/right boundaries carefully.
hints: Stop when left becomes greater than right.
Only one half needs further recursion.

examples.explanation: 7 is found at index 3.

title: Greatest Common Divisor
description: Given two non-negative integers a and b, compute their greatest common divisor recursively.
intendedApproachSummary: Use Euclid's algorithm: gcd(a, b) = gcd(b, a % b), with gcd(a, 0) = a.
wrongApproachHints: Do not recurse on unchanged arguments.
The base case must trigger when the second value becomes 0.
hints: Replace the larger problem with the remainder form.
Look for the stopping condition when one number is exhausted.

title: Generate All Subsequences
description: Given an array of length n, generate all subsequences using recursion and backtracking.
edgeCases: Array with repeated values if duplicates are not removed
intendedApproachSummary: At each index, branch into two choices: include the current element or exclude it.
wrongApproachHints: Missing one branch means half the subsequences disappear.
If using a shared path list, undo the pick before exploring the exclude branch.
hints: Every element creates a yes/no choice.
Think of a decision tree.

examples.explanation: Each element can be either taken or skipped.

title: Subset Sum Exists
description: Given an array and a target sum, determine whether any subsequence has sum equal to the target.
outputFormat: Return true if a valid subsequence exists, otherwise false
intendedApproachSummary: At each index, either include the current value in the sum or skip it, then recurse on the next index.
wrongApproachHints: Stopping too early can miss valid later combinations.
Be careful not to mutate the running sum incorrectly across branches.
hints: This is include/exclude again, but now you track a running sum.
A true answer can short-circuit the search.

examples.explanation: The subsequence [2,3] sums to 5.

title: Combination Sum
description: Given distinct candidate numbers and a target, return all unique combinations where the chosen numbers sum to the target. A number may be chosen multiple times.
edgeCases: A candidate equals the target exactly
intendedApproachSummary: Try taking the current candidate again or move to the next candidate, and backtrack after each choice.
wrongApproachHints: If you always move forward after taking a number, you cannot reuse it.
Remember to remove the last number after each recursive exploration.
hints: This is a choice tree with reuse.
Stop when the remaining target becomes negative or zero.

examples.explanation: Both [2,2,3] and [7] sum to 7.

title: Generate Permutations
description: Generate all permutations of a list of distinct integers using recursion.
inputFormat: An integer array nums with distinct values
intendedApproachSummary: Build the permutation one position at a time by choosing an unused element, recursing, then undoing the choice.
wrongApproachHints: If you do not mark used elements, the same value may appear twice in one permutation.
Undo both the path and used marker when backtracking.
hints: Track which elements are already in the current path.
A full path becomes one answer.

examples.explanation: Three distinct numbers produce 3! = 6 permutations.

title: Tower of Hanoi
description: Print the sequence of moves required to transfer n disks from the source rod to the destination rod using an auxiliary rod.
outputFormat: Print the sequence of valid moves
intendedApproachSummary: Move n - 1 disks out of the way, move the largest disk, then move the n - 1 disks onto the destination.
wrongApproachHints: The auxiliary and destination rods swap roles between the two recursive calls.
Do not try to move the largest disk before clearing smaller ones.
hints: Think about how to free the largest disk first.
The structure repeats on n - 1 disks.

examples.explanation: You first move the smaller disk, then the larger one, then restore the smaller disk on top.

title: Josephus Problem
description: Given n people standing in a circle and a step size k, return the index of the survivor when every kth person is removed.
outputFormat: Return the safe position (0-based or 1-based depending on your convention)
intendedApproachSummary: Use the recursive relation for the survivor in smaller circles, then map it back to the current size.
wrongApproachHints: Brute force simulation is possible but misses the intended recurrence.
Be consistent about 0-based or 1-based indexing.
hints: Think about the answer for n - 1 people first.
How does adding one more person shift the answer?

examples.explanation: With 0-based indexing, person 2 survives.

title: Climbing Stairs with Memoization
description: Count the number of distinct ways to climb n stairs if you can take 1 or 2 steps at a time. Use recursion with memoization.
outputFormat: Return the total number of ways
intendedApproachSummary: The state repeats like Fibonacci. Use a memo table so each stair count is solved only once.
wrongApproachHints: Plain recursion repeats the same states many times.
Cache results before returning them.
hints: ways(n) depends on ways(n-1) and ways(n-2).
Memoization removes repeated work.

title: Tribonacci with Memoization
description: Return the nth Tribonacci number, where each term is the sum of the previous three terms. Use recursion with memoization.
intendedApproachSummary: This is tree recursion with three branches, but memoization collapses repeated states into linear work.
wrongApproachHints: Do not recompute the same n repeatedly.
Make sure the first three base cases are correct.
hints: Define the first three values clearly.
Cache once, reuse many times.

examples.explanation: If T0=0, T1=1, T2=1, then T3=2 and T4=4.

title: Merge Sort
description: Sort an array using merge sort and recursion.
intendedApproachSummary: Recursively split the range into halves, sort each half, then merge the sorted halves.
wrongApproachHints: The merge step is required; recursion alone does not sort.
Take care with mid and temporary storage.
hints: Think in terms of left half, right half, then merge.
A range of length 1 is already sorted.

examples.explanation: Each half is sorted recursively, then the two sorted halves are merged.

title: Quick Sort
description: Implement quick sort recursively by partitioning the array around a pivot.
intendedApproachSummary: Partition the array around a pivot so smaller values go left and larger values go right, then recurse on both sides.
wrongApproachHints: Partition boundaries are easy to get wrong.
Do not recurse on invalid or empty ranges.
hints: First get partitioning correct on small examples.
The pivot ends in its final sorted position after partitioning.

examples.explanation: Each partition step places one pivot correctly and splits the remaining work.

title: Sudoku Solver
description: Fill a partially completed 9x9 Sudoku board using recursive backtracking.
constraints: The board size is fixed at 9x9
inputFormat: A 9x9 board with empty cells marked
outputFormat: Return or print a valid completed board
intendedApproachSummary: Find an empty cell, try every valid digit, recurse, and backtrack immediately when a placement leads to a dead end.
wrongApproachHints: If you forget to undo a placement, future branches start from a corrupted board.
Validity checks should happen before recursing deeper.
hints: Choose one empty cell at a time.
Prune quickly when a digit violates row, column, or box rules.

examples.explanation: The solver explores valid candidate placements recursively until the board is complete.

title: N-Queens
description: Place n queens on an n x n chessboard so that no two queens attack each other. Return all valid board arrangements.
edgeCases: n = 2 and n = 3 have no solution
intendedApproachSummary: Place one queen row by row, only continue with positions that are safe, and backtrack after exploring each choice.
wrongApproachHints: If you do not check diagonals, invalid boards slip through.
Always remove the queen before trying the next column.
hints: Think row by row.
Columns and diagonals are the real safety checks.

examples.explanation: There are exactly two distinct non-attacking arrangements for 4 queens.
